admin_ui_rules.purpose: Design and interaction rules for admin dashboard interfaces.
global_principles: Admin interfaces prioritize clarity, speed, dense information display, and task completion over decoration.
The admin dashboard must feel stable, predictable, and highly scannable.
Every major admin section must have a clear purpose and visible hierarchy.
Use shared global design tokens and component systems whenever possible.
Avoid creating one-off admin-only visual patterns unless they solve a real workflow problem.
Preserve consistent interaction patterns across tabs, cards, tables, forms, and dialogs.
Prefer compact but readable layouts over spacious marketing-style layouts.
Do not use decorative animation that slows down task-oriented work.

rules: The page title must be the strongest text element on the page.
The active tab context must clearly indicate which workspace the user is in.
Card headers must be visually stronger than card body text but weaker than the page title.
Utility or support panels such as logs must not visually overpower the main workspace.
Secondary information must remain clearly separated from the main task area.
Use a slightly wider container than public-facing content pages.
Avoid excessively wide unreadable tables by using scroll containers where necessary.
Use tighter vertical spacing than public marketing pages.
Reduce card and table padding where appropriate while preserving readability.
Do not compress controls so much that tap/click accuracy suffers.
Dense layouts must still preserve grouping and separation.
Use spacing tokens only.
Prefer compact admin spacing such as var(--sp-2), var(--sp-3), var(--sp-4).
Avoid raw rem or px spacing unless tied to an explicit token.
Maintain consistent spacing rhythm between cards, form groups, and data tables.

responsive_rules: Admin grids must collapse predictably on smaller screens.
info-grid and form-row must stack to a single column on narrow widths.
Do not allow cards or form controls to overflow horizontally.
Use horizontal scrolling for dense tables on small screens instead of uncontrolled wrapping.
Maintain clear touch targets on smaller devices.

tabs.purpose: Top-level workspace switching within the admin dashboard.
visual_rules: Inactive tabs must look clickable but visually muted.
Active tabs must have a strong indicator such as accent border-bottom and stronger text color.
Hover state must improve discoverability without competing with the active state.
Focus-visible state must be clearly visible.
interaction_rules: Tab switching should feel immediate and stable.
Avoid large layout jumps when switching tabs.
Keep tab labels short and literal.
Do not overload tabs with badges or noisy visuals unless task-critical.
keyboard_rules: Left and right arrow keys should move between tabs where feasible.
Enter or Space should activate the focused tab.
Focus order must remain logical after switching tabs.
state_sync_rules: URL hash or parameter sync is allowed as a progressive enhancement.
If URL-based tab sync is implemented, it must not break no-JS or default tab behavior.
Default fallback tab must be predictable.

cards.purpose: Primary grouping containers for admin content.
visual_rules: Use global card variables for background, radius, and shadow.
Interactive cards may use a subtle hover lift or border emphasis.
Non-interactive cards should remain visually stable.
Card headers must be clearly separated from card bodies.
density_rules: Card padding should be tighter than public pages.
Cards that contain tables or forms may use compact padding to maximize usable space.
Do not stack too many nested card layers.

principles: Admin forms must optimize for speed and accuracy.
Labels must be visible and clear.
Required fields must be understandable before submission.
Validation must help recovery, not just block progress.

rules: Use the shared global input styles.
Use consistent padding, border, radius, and focus ring.
Do not create custom admin-only input visuals unless functionally necessary.
Select controls must match input sizing and visual language.
Use clear selected states and visible focus treatment.
Textareas must match the global form language.
Allow vertical resizing only if it does not break layout.

layout_rules: Use form rows and groups with predictable spacing.
On mobile, stack form groups cleanly.
Do not create irregular field widths without reason.
validation_rules: Required fields must communicate their requirement clearly.
Use :invalid styles or equivalent validation states consistently.
Validation feedback must not rely on color alone.
Display inline validation messages near the relevant field when possible.
Show success and error messages using consistent status components.
submit_rules: Primary form submission must be visually clear.
Secondary actions such as cancel or reset must not compete with submit.
Disable submit only when the reason is clear or user-understandable.

button_semantics.principle: Use semantic styling sparingly to maintain visual discipline.

rules: Use one dominant primary button style.
Do not create multiple competing primary actions in the same context.
Must be visually quieter than primary.
Supports the main task without competing.
Use sparingly.
Do not turn every positive action green.
Reserve for actions needing caution.
Do not use for ordinary editing tasks.
Use only for destructive actions.
Danger actions should require confirmation where appropriate.
Use tertiary or ghost styles.
Utility actions must not visually overpower task actions.

principles: Tables are work surfaces, not decorative components.
Dense information must remain readable and scannable.
Header clarity and action predictability are critical.
structure_rules: Use a scroll wrapper with overflow-x auto when needed.
Use sticky headers when table height and scrolling justify it.
Headers must remain visually distinct from rows.
Action controls should appear in a predictable last column when possible.
Numeric values such as price or counts should align right.
Use controlled text overflow for long content.
Prefer truncation with title/tooltips or wrap rules depending on context.
row_rules: Use subtle row separation or striping.
Hover highlight must be visible but restrained.
Selected or active rows must have a clear state.
Rows must not feel cramped even in dense mode.
mobile_rules: Prefer horizontal scrolling over chaotic wrapping for data-dense tables.
Maintain action visibility on small screens.
Do not let action buttons wrap to multiple lines if it harms readability.
empty_rules: Empty tables must be replaced or supplemented by a meaningful empty state.
Do not show blank table shells without guidance.

rules: Every major admin area must define its state behavior.
Loading states should indicate that work is in progress without causing panic.
Empty states should guide the user toward the next meaningful action.
Success states should confirm completion clearly but briefly.
Error states should explain the issue and recovery path where possible.

progress_and_logs.purpose: Developer-oriented visibility into background or ongoing admin tasks.
visual_rules: Progress/log panels may use a terminal-inspired style.
Use darker surfaces and monospace typography.
Maintain readable contrast and sufficient line spacing.
Do not let the log panel dominate the page visually.
behavior_rules: New logs should appear predictably at the end.
Auto-scroll to bottom is recommended when the user is already near the bottom.
Allow manual scroll override so the user can inspect earlier logs.
Use aria-live politely for important live updates when appropriate.

login_overlay.purpose: Secure access gate before entering the dashboard.
layout_rules: Center the login box with stable spacing.
Use a constrained width that works on mobile and desktop.
Do not allow the overlay contents to overflow narrow screens.
interaction_rules: Inputs must use shared focus-visible states.
Loading state must be visible on submit.
Error state must be clearly communicated.
Disabled state must be visually clear.
Enter key submission should work when appropriate.
accessibility_rules: Focus should move into the login overlay when shown.
Focus should not be lost behind the overlay.
Error messages must be readable without relying only on color.

rules: Destructive actions should trigger confirmation dialogs when accidental activation would be costly.
Confirmation dialogs must have clear primary and cancel actions.
Dialog copy must explain the consequence plainly.
Do not use confirmation for trivial reversible actions.
Use consistent status components for success, error, warning, and neutral information.
Messages must be concise and actionable.
Icons may support status meaning but must not be the only signal.
Status text should explain what happened and what to do next when relevant.

principles: Admin interfaces must remain efficient for keyboard and assistive technology users.
Color must not be the only carrier of meaning.
rules: Use :focus-visible for all interactive elements.
Ensure keyboard navigation works for tabs, forms, buttons, and dialogs.
Use aria-live for dynamic status updates where appropriate.
Keep labels explicit and accessible.
Ensure contrast meets readable standards.

anti_patterns: Huge admin paddings that waste working space
Multiple competing button colors in the same area
Logs that visually overpower the main workspace
Validation that only says 'invalid' without explanation
Tabs that rely on color alone
Embedded page-specific styles that bypass the design system
implementation_notes_for_ai: Refactor UI structure before rebinding behavior logic.
Do not invent new admin components unless necessary.
Reuse global tokens, buttons, cards, forms, and state styles first.
When uncertain, choose compact clarity over visual novelty.
Preserve predictable action placement and hierarchy at all times.
